standard_description: Solve real world and mathematical problems involving perimeters of polygons, including finding the perimeter given the side lengths, finding an unknown side length, and exhibiting rectangles with the same perimeter and different areas or with the same area and different perimeters.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
a.   Create as many rectangles as you can with a perimeter of 16 units. Find the area of each rectangle that you found. Then record the length, width, perimeter, and area of each rectangle in the table below:
###TABLE0###
b.   What do you notice about the rectangles you created? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
What is the greatest area you can make with a rectangle that has a perimeter of 20 units?

Problem 1
The shape below has a perimeter of 30 feet.
###IMAGE0###
What is the length of the side that is missing a number?
Problem 2
Tessa ropes off a rectangular part of her lawn to create a play area for her dog. She uses 32 yards of rope. What is the missing side length of the play area?
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
Claudia is redecorating her bedroom, which is in the shape of a rectangle that is 8 feet long and 9 feet wide. She needs to buy new carpeting and wants to put baseboards around the border of her bedroom.
a.   How much carpeting should Claudia order?
b.   How much baseboard should Claudia order?
Problem 2
The length of a rectangle is 6 cm and its perimeter is 16 cm. What is the area of the rectangle in square centimeters?

Problem 1
a.   Create as many rectangles as you can with an area of 12 square units. Find the perimeter of each rectangle that you created. Then record the length, width, area, and perimeter of each rectangle in the table below:
###TABLE0###
b.   What do you notice about the rectangles you created? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
What is the greatest perimeter you can make with a rectangle that has an area of 24 square units?

Problem 3
Pre-unit
Draw a rectangle on the grid.
What is the perimeter of the rectangle?
###IMAGE0###

Using your tetrominoes, make a rectangle that has an area of 24 square units. You can use the same tetromino shape more than once. Then draw your rectangle on the grid below, showing how the tetrominoes fit together.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Maureen wants to wrap ribbon around her mirror and around her picture frame. Will she need more ribbon for her mirror or her picture frame? Both are shown below.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Outline both shapes below in one color. Then color the inside of both shapes in another color.
###IMAGE1###
a.   Which color represents the perimeter of the shape? How do you know? What does the other color represent?
b.    Which shape has greater perimeter? How do you know?
c.   Which shape has greater area? How do you know?

Problem 1
Gabrielle put a frame around a clock that is in the shape of a regular hexagon. If Gabrielle used 42 centimeters of framing, how long is each side of the clock?
Problem 2
The perimeter of a rectangular index card is 26 inches. Its length is 5 inches. What is its width?

Problem 1
Chris is replacing the fence around his rectangular backyard. Chris’s drawing of the backyard is shown below. Chris measured two of the side lengths and labeled them in his drawing.
###IMAGE0###
How much fencing does he need to buy?
Problem 2
The following shape is a regular polygon.
###IMAGE1###
What is the perimeter, in centimeters, of the shape?

Problem 1
A rectangular notecard has a perimeter of 20 inches. The length of the notecard is 4 inches. What is the width of the notecard?
Problem 2

Mrs. Kozlow put a border around an 8-foot by 12-foot rectangular bulletin board. How many feet of border did Mrs. Kozlow use?

The Pentagon is a building in Washington, D.C. that houses the Department of Defense. The building is in the shape of a regular pentagon. Jason built a model of the Pentagon for a social studies project. He made each outside wall 33 centimeters long. What is the perimeter of Jason’s model Pentagon?

Hiram wants to frame a square-sized painting. One side length of the square painting measures 7 inches. What is the total length of frame Hiram will need?

Problem 1
The area of a rectangular bulletin board is 20 square feet. The length of the bulletin board is 5 feet. What is the perimeter of the bulletin board?
Problem 2
Mrs. Rothschild wants to put up a new bulletin board outside of her classroom. She wants the bulletin board to be 4 feet tall and 7 feet long. The bulletin board will be covered with cork and will have a metal frame around it.
a.   How much metal framing does Mrs. Rothschild need?

Edris wants to build a rectangular garden in his backyard to grow vegetables. He buys 60 yards of fencing to put around the garden to keep animals out. He wants the garden to be 12 feet long. What should the width of his garden be so that he uses all of the fencing he bought?

khan_exercises.title: Area & perimeter of rectangles word problems
khan_exercises.description: Find the missing side length of a rectangle when given its perimeter or area.  Compare perimeters and areas of rectangles.
topic_path: Module 7: Geometry and measurement word problems
Topic D: Problem solving with perimeter and area
Area & perimeter of rectangles word problems

items.question_content:
Margo wants to build a rectangular fence around her garden, and she has $24$ meters of wood.

**Which of these dimensions will give Margo the largest area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the area of each rectangle to see which one has the largest area.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

###$8$ meters by $4$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD8\text{ m}\times\blueD4\text{ m} = 32\text{ square meters}$

###$10$ meters by $2$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD{10}\text{ m}\times\blueD2\text{ m} = 20\text{ square meters}$

###$6$ meters by $6$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD{6}\text{ m}\times\blueD6\text{ m} = 36\text{ square meters}$
The garden with the dimensions of $6\text{ meters}$ by $6\text{ meters}$ has the largest area.

items.question_content:
Jordan wants to create a rectangular border around his classroom bulletin board, and he has $30$ meters of border.

**Which of these dimensions will give Jordan the largest area?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the area of each rectangle to see which one has the largest area.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

###$10$ meters by $5$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD{10}\text{ m}\times\blueD5\text{ m} = 50\text{ square meters}$

###$7$ meters by $8$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD{8}\text{ m}\times\blueD7\text{ m} = 56\text{ square meters}$

###$12$ meters by $3$ meters


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Area}=\greenD{12}\text{ m}\times\blueD3\text{ m} = 36\text{ square meters}$
The bulletin board with the dimensions of $8\text{ meters}$ by $7\text{ meters}$ has the largest area.

items.question_content:
Chloe has enough sand to fill a rectangular sandbox with an area of $36$ square units. She wants the outer edges of the sandbox to use as little material as possible.

**Which dimensions will give Chloe the smallest perimeter?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's find the perimeter of each rectangle to see which one has the smallest perimeter.

$\text{Perimeter} = \text{length} + \text{width} +  \text{length} + \text{width}$

###$6$ units by $6$ units


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter}=\greenD{6}\text{ u}+\blueD6\text{ u} + \greenD{6}\text{ u}+\blueD6\text{ u} = 24\text{ units}$

###$9$ units by $4$ units

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter}=\greenD{9}\text{ u}+\blueD4\text{ u} + \greenD{9}\text{ u}+\blueD4\text{ u} = 26\text{ units}$

###$12$ units by $3$ units


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter}=\greenD{12}\text{ u}+\blueD3\text{ u} + \greenD{12}\text{ u}+\blueD3\text{ u} = 30\text{ units}$
$24\text{ u} < 26\text{ u}< 30\text{ u}$
A rectanglar sandbox with the dimensions of $6\text{ units}$ by $6\text{ units}$ has the smallest perimeter.

items.question_content:
The area of a square is $144$ square centimeters.  

**What is the length of one side of the square?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] centimeters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills. 

$\text{Area of a square} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

What number can we multiply by itself to get $\goldE{144}$?

$\goldE{144}=\greenD{s}\times\blueD{s}$
The length of one side of the square is $\greenD{12}$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
A square has an area of $100$ square meters.

**What is the perimeter of the square?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills. 

$\text{Area of a square} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

What number can we multiply by itself to get $\goldE{100}$?

$\goldE{100}=\greenD{s}\times\blueD{s}$

The length of each side is $10$ meters.

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ side}} + \blueD{\text{ side}} +  \greenD{\text{ side}} + \blueD{\text{ side}}\\\\
&=\greenD{10} + \blueD{10} + \greenD{10} + \blueD{10}\\\\
&=40
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the square is $40$ meters.

items.question_content:
A square has an area of $49$ square meters.

**What is the perimeter of the square?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills. 

$\text{Area of a square} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

What number can we multiply by itself to get $\goldE{49}$?

$\goldE{49}=\greenD{s}\times\blueD{s}$

The length of each side is $7$ meters.

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ side}} + \blueD{\text{ side}} +  \greenD{\text{ side}} + \blueD{\text{ side}}\\\\
&=\greenD7 + \blueD7 + \greenD7 + \blueD7\\\\
&=28
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the square is $28$ meters.

items.question_content:
Steve built a square hamster pen that has a perimeter of $240$ centimeters.

**What is the length of one side of Steve's hamster pen?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] centimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter of the square is $\purpleD{240}\text{ cm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{240} &=  \greenD{s}  + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{240} &= 4 \times s
\end{align}$
What number can we multiply by $4$ to get $\purpleD{240}$?

We can divide to find the side length.  

$\purpleD{240}\div4= \blueD{\text{s}}$
One side of the hamster pen is $\blueD{60}$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
The perimeter of Jonah's square backyard is $56$ meters.

**What is the area of Jonah's backyard?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] square meters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter of the square is $\purpleD{56}\text{ m}$.

[[☃ image 1]]



$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{56} &=  \greenD{s}  + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{56} &= 4 \times s
\end{align}$
What number can we multiply by $4$ to get $\purpleD{56}$?

Now, we can find the area. 

$\begin{align}
\text{Area} &= \text{side} \times \text{side}\\\\
&= \blueD{s} \times \blueD{s}\\\\
&= \blueD{14} \times \blueD{14}\\\\
&= 196\text{ square meters}
\end{align}$
The area of Jonah's backyard is $196$ square meters.

items.question_content:
A square has a perimeter of $36$ millimeters.

**What is the area of the square?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] square millimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter of the square is $\purpleD{36}\text{ mm}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{36} &=  \greenD{s}  + \greenD{s} + \blueD{s} + \blueD{s}\\\\
\purpleD{36} &= 4 \times s
\end{align}$
What number can we multiply by $4$ to get $\purpleD{36}$?

Now, we can find the area. 

$\begin{align}
\text{Area} &= \text{side} \times \text{side}\\\\
&= \blueD{s} \times \blueD{s}\\\\
&= \blueD{9} \times \blueD{9}\\\\
&= 81\text{ square mm}
\end{align}$
The area of the square is $81$ square millimeters.

items.question_content:
The area of a rectangle is $117$ square meters.  The width is $9$ meters.

**What is the length of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$

The **$\goldE{\text{area}}$** of the rectangle is $\goldE{117}$ square meters and the $\blueD{\text{width}}$ is $\blueD{9}$ meters.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}} &= \greenD{\ell}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{117} &= \greenD{\ell}\times\blueD{9}\\\\
\goldE{117}\div\blueD9&=\greenD{\ell}
\end{align}$
The length of the rectangle is $\greenD{13}$ meters.

items.question_content:
Jason built a rectangular tool shed that is $8$ meters wide and has an area of $96$ square meters.  

**What is the length of Jason’s tool shed?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$

The **$\goldE{\text{area}}$** of the rectangle is $\goldE{96}$ square meters and the $\blueD{\text{width}}$ is $\blueD{8}$ meters.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}} &= \greenD{\ell}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{96} &= \greenD{\ell}\times\blueD{8}\\\\
\goldE{96}\div\blueD8&=\greenD{\ell}
\end{align}$
The length of the tool shed is $\greenD{12}$ meters.

items.question_content:
The area of a rectangle is $28$ square meters.  The length is $7$ meters.

**What is the width of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{height}$

The **$\goldE{\text{area}}$** of the rectangle is $\goldE{28}$ square meters and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $\greenD{7}$ meters.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}} &= \greenD{7}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{28} &= \greenD{7}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{28}\div\greenD7&=\blueD{w}
\end{align}$
The width of the rectangle is $\blueD{4}$ meters.

items.question_content:
Elise built a large rectangular vegetable garden that is $9$ meters long and has an area of $72$ square meters.  

**What is the perimeter of Elise’s vegetable garden?**

[[☃ input-number 2]] meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$

The **$\goldE{\text{area}}$** of the rectangle is $\goldE{72}$ square meters and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $\greenD{9}$ meters.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}} &= \greenD{9}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{72} &= \greenD{9}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{72}\div\greenD9&=\blueD{w}\\\\
\blueD8&=\blueD{w}
\end{align}$

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}} +  \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}}\\\\
&=\greenD9 + \blueD8 + \greenD9 + \blueD8\\\\
&=34
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the garden is $34$ meters.

items.question_content:
The area of a rectangle is $42$ square millimeters.  The length is $7$ millimeters.

**What is the perimeter of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] millimeters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills.

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$

The **$\goldE{\text{area}}$** of the rectangle is $\goldE{42}$ square millimeters and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $\greenD{7}$ millimeters.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{area}} &= \greenD{7}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{42} &= \greenD{7}\times\blueD{w}\\\\
\goldE{42}\div\greenD7&=\blueD{w}\\\\
\blueD6&=\blueD{w}
\end{align}$

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}} +  \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}}\\\\
&=\greenD7 + \blueD6 + \greenD7 + \blueD6\\\\
&=26
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the rectangle is $26$ millimeters.

items.question_content:
Judy built a rectangular patio that is $9$ meters wide and has a perimeter of $40$ meters.  

**What is the length of Judy's patio?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter is $\purpleD{40}\text{ m}$ and the width is $\blueD{9}\text{ m}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{l} + \blueD{w} + \greenD{l} + \blueD{w}\\\\
\purpleD{40} &= \greenD{\ell} + \blueD9 +  \greenD{\ell} + \blueD9 \\\\
\purpleD{40} &=  \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell} + \blueD{18}\\\\
22 &= \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell}
\end{align}$
What number can we add twice to get $22$?
The length of the patio is $\greenD{11}$ meters.

items.question_content:
The perimeter of a rectangle is $90$ centimeters.  The length is $27$ centimeters.

**What is the width of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] centimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter is $\purpleD{90}\text{ cm}$ and the length is $\greenD{27}\text{ cm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{l} + \blueD{w} + \greenD{l} + \blueD{w}\\\\
\purpleD{90} &= \greenD{27} + \blueD{w} +  \greenD{27} + \blueD{w} \\\\
\purpleD{90} &=  \greenD{54} + \blueD{w} + \blueD{w}\\\\
36 &=  \blueD{w} + \blueD{w}
\end{align}$
What number can we add twice to get $36$?
The width of the rectangle is $\blueD{18}$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
Lisa built a rectangular flower garden that is $4$ meters wide and has a perimeter of $26$ meters.  

**What is the length of Lisa's flower garden?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] meters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter is $\purpleD{26}\text{ m}$ and the width is $\blueD{4}\text{ m}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{l} + \blueD{w} + \greenD{l} + \blueD{w}\\\\
\purpleD{26} &= \greenD{\ell} + \blueD4 +  \greenD{\ell} + \blueD4 \\\\
\purpleD{26} &=  \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell} + \blueD{8}\\\\
18&= \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell}
\end{align}$
What number can we add twice to get $18$?
The length of the flower garden is $\greenD{9}$ meters.

items.question_content:
The perimeter of a rectangle is $20$ centimeters.  The length is $6$ centimeters.

**What is the area of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] square centimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter is $\purpleD{20}\text{ cm}$ and the length is $\greenD{6}\text{ cm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{l} + \blueD{w} + \greenD{l} + \blueD{w}\\\\
\purpleD{20} &= \greenD{6} + \blueD{w} +  \greenD{6} + \blueD{w} \\\\
\purpleD{20} &=  \blueD{w}  + \blueD{w} + \greenD{12}\\\\
8&= \blueD{w} + \blueD{w}
\end{align}$

What number can we add twice to get $8$?

The width of the rectangle is $\blueD{4}$ centimeters.
The area of the rectangle is $24$ square centimeters.

items.question_content:
The perimeter of a rectangle is $32$ centimeters.  The width is $7$ centimeters.

**What is the area of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] square centimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The perimeter is $\purpleD{32}\text{ cm}$ and the width is $\blueD{7}\text{ cm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{P} &= \greenD{l} + \blueD{w} + \greenD{l} + \blueD{w}\\\\
\purpleD{32} &= \greenD{\ell} + \blueD7 +  \greenD{\ell} + \blueD7 \\\\
\purpleD{32} &=  \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell} + \blueD{14}\\\\
18&= \greenD{\ell}  + \greenD{\ell}
\end{align}$

What number can we add twice to get $18$?

The length of the rectangle is $\greenD{9}$ centimeters.
The area of the rectangle is $63$ square centimeters.

items.question_content:
A rectangle has a width of $3$ units.  The rectangle's length is $5$ times its width.  

**What is the area of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] square units
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills. 

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$
The $\blueD{\text{ width}}$ is $\blueD{3}\text{ units}$ and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $5$ times the $\blueD{\text{width}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $5\times\blueD3=\greenD{15}$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can find the area.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{Area}} &= \greenD{l} \times \blueD{w}\\\\ 
&= \greenD{15}\times \blueD{3}\\\\
&=45
\end{align}$
The area of the rectangle is $45$ square units.

items.question_content:
A rectangular playground is $8$ meters wide.  It is $3$ times as long as it is wide.

**What is the area of the playground?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] square meters
hints:
**Area** is the amount of space a flat shape fills. 

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{width} \times \text{length}$

The $\blueD{\text{width}}$ is $\blueD{8}\text{ meters}$ and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $3$ times the $\blueD{\text{width}}$.




[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $3\times\blueD{8}=\greenD{24}\text{ meters}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can find the area.

$\begin{align}
\goldE{\text{Area}} &= \greenD{l} \times \blueD{w}\\\\ 
&= \greenD{24}\times \blueD{8}\\\\
&=192
\end{align}$
The area of the playground is $192$ square meters.

items.question_content:
A rectangle has a width of $10$ centimeters.  The rectangle's length is $4$ times its width.  

**What is the perimeter of the rectangle?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] centimeters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The $\blueD{\text{width}}$ is $\blueD{10}\text{ cm}$ and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $4$ times the $\blueD{\text{width}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $4\times\blueD{10}=\greenD{40}\text{ cm}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}} +  \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}}\\\\
&=\greenD{40}+ \blueD{10} + \greenD{40} + \blueD{10}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the rectangle is $100$ centimeters.

items.question_content:
A rectangular sandbox has a width of $5$ units.  The sandbox is $5$ times as long as it is wide.  

**What is the perimeter of the sandbox?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]] units
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around a closed figure. 

We can find the perimeter of a shape by adding all the side lengths.

The $\blueD{\text{width}}$ is $\blueD{5}\text{ units}$ and the $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $5$ times the $\blueD{\text{width}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenD{\text{length}}$ is $5\times\blueD{5}=\greenD{25}$ units.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, we can find the perimeter.  

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}} +  \greenD{\text{ length}} + \blueD{\text{ width}}\\\\
&=\greenD{25}+ \blueD{5} + \greenD{25} + \blueD{5}\\\\
&=60
\end{align}$
The perimeter of the sandbox is $60$ units.

khan_exercises.title: Area and perimeter word problems
khan_exercises.description: Solve 1- and 2-step word problems involving area of rectangles and perimeter of polygons.

items.question_content:
Tom wants new carpeting for his bedroom. His room is a $9$ meters by $7$ meters rectangle. 

**How much carpeting does he need to buy to cover his entire bedroom floor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Tom wants to find the amount of carpet it will take to **cover** his entire bedroom floor, so he is finding the **area**.
Tom needs $63$ square meters to cover his bedroom floor.

items.question_content:
Gina is getting a new bathroom mirror. The rectangular mirror measures $6$ meters by $8$ meters.

**What is the amount of space the mirror will cover on the wall?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Gina wants to find the amount of flat space the mirror will **cover** on the wall, so she is finding the **area**.
The mirror will cover $48$ square meters of the wall.

items.question_content:
Tomlin is removing the wallpaper. The wallpaper covers a $5$ meter by $4$ meter rectangular wall.

**How much wallpaper will Tomlin remove?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Tomlin wants to find the amount of wallpaper that **covers** his wall, so he is finding the **area**.
Tomlin will remove $20$ square meters of wallpaper.

items.question_content:
Gia is painting her wall. $1$ liter of paint can cover $10$ square meters. She has a rectangular wall that measures $4$ meters by $5$ meters. 

**How many liters of paint will Gia need to cover her wall?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Gia wants to find the amount of paint it will take to **cover** her entire wall, so she is finding the **area**.
Gia will need $2$ liters.

items.question_content:
Jose wants to cover his rectangular bathroom floor in tile. The floor measures $3$ units by $5$ units. Each tile is $1$ square unit and costs $\$4$.

**How much will it cost Jose to cover his entire bathroom floor in tile?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Jose wants to find the amount of tile it will take to **cover** his bathroom floor, so he is finding the **area**.

Now, Jose needs to find out how much it will cost to cover his $15$ square unit bathroom floor with tile.

If $1$ tile costs $\$4$, how much will it cost him to get $15$ tiles?
It will cost Jose $\$60$ to cover his entire bathroom floor.

items.question_content:
Sally is building a garden. She plans to build a rectangular garden measuring $6$ meters by $3$ meters. She wants to put a net over top of the garden to keep the birds out. She has $12$ square meters of netting in her garage.

**How much more netting will Sally need to cover her whole garden?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Sally wants to find the amount of netting it will take to **cover** her garden, so she is finding the **area**.

Now, Sally needs to find out how much more netting she will need if she already has $12$ square meters of netting.

If Sally needs to cover $18$ square meters but already has $12$ square meters, how much more netting will she need?
$18\text{ square meters} - 12\text{ square meters} = 6\text{ square meters}$
Sally needs $6$ square meters of netting.

items.question_content:
A restaurant has a rectangular patio section that is $8$ meters wide by $6$ meters long. They want to use fencing to enclose the patio.

**How much fencing will they need to go around their new patio?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The restaurant wants to find out the amount of fencing to go **around** the patio, so they want to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD8\text{ m} +\purpleC6\text{ m}+ \greenD8\text{ m}+\purpleC6\text{ m}$
The restaurant will need $28\text{ m}$ of fencing to around their patio.

items.question_content:
Natalie runs around the outside of a square park in her city. Each side of the park measures $20$ meters. 

**How far did Natalie run around the park?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{ meters}$
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Natalie is going to run **around** the park, so she wants to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{20}\text{ m} +\purpleC{20}\text{ m}+ \greenD{20}\text{ m}+\purpleC{20}\text{ m}$
Natalie ran $80\text{ m}$ around the park.

items.question_content:
Christen has to run around the rectangular soccer field during practice. The soccer field measures $60$ meters long by $45$ meters wide.

**How far did Christen run?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{ meters}$
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Christian ran **around** the soccer field, so she ran around the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{60}\text{ m} +\purpleC{45}\text{ m}+ \greenD{60}\text{ m}+\purpleC{45}\text{ m}$
Christen ran $210\text{ m}$.

items.question_content:
A construction company is starting to build a new house. They need to put tape around the site to begin. The new home site is $20$ meters wide by $40$ meter long rectangle. The company already has $100$ meters of tape.  

**How much more tape does the company need to tape the site?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The construction company wants to find out the amount of tape needed to go **around** the outside of the construction site, so they want to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{20}\text{ m} +\purpleC{40}\text{ m}+ \greenD{20}\text{ m}+\purpleC{40}\text{ m} = 120\text{ m}$

The construction company already has $100\text{ m}$ of tape but needs $120\text{ m}$. How much do they still need?

$120\text{ m} - 100\text{ m} = 20\text{ m}$
The construction company needs $20\text{ m}$ of tape.

items.question_content:
A rectangular football field measures $50$ meters by $25$ meters. The football team has to run around the field $4$ times during warm ups.

**How many meters does the team run during warm ups?**
  
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The football team has to run **around** the outside of the football, so let's find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{25}\text{ m} +\purpleC{50}\text{ m}+ \greenD{25}\text{ m}+\purpleC{50}\text{ m} = 150\text{ m}$

The team has to run around the field $4$ times.

$150\text{ m} + 150\text{ m}  + 150\text{ m}  + 150\text{ m} = 600\text{ m}$
The team runs $600\text{ m}$ during warm up.

items.question_content:
Steph needs to help her parents put a fence around their pool. They want the fence to be square and want each side to measure $6$ meters. They already have $10$ meters of fencing. 

**How many more meters of fencing should they buy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Steph is putting a fence **around** her parent's pool, so she needs to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{6}\text{ m} +\purpleC{6}\text{ m}+ \greenD{6}\text{ m}+\purpleC{6}\text{ m} = 24\text{ m}$

Her family already has $10$ meters of fencing. How much more fencing will they need?

$24\text{ m} -10\text{ m}= 14\text{ m}$
Steph's family needs $14$ meters of fencing.
